Chapter 4 Multi-Head Attention A single attention mechanism may be limited in its capacity to capture different types of relationships simultaneously. Multi-head attention addresses this limitation by computing multiple attention functions in parallel, each with independent parameters, allowing the model to attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions.
4.1 Motivation and Architecture Consider the task of understanding the sentence “The animal didn’t cross the street because it was too tired.” The pronoun “it” could potentially refer to either “animal” or “street,” though in this context it refers to “animal.” Determining this requires understanding: 1. Syntactic structure (subject-verb relationships) 2. Semantic plausibility (animals get tired, streets don’t) 3. Long-range dependencies (the pronoun appears far from its antecedent) A single attention mechanism must balance these different types of information. Multi-head attention allows different heads to specialize in different aspects, some focusing on syntax, others on semantics, others on positional patterns.
4.1.1 Formal Definition Definition 4.1 (Multi-Head Attention). Multi-head attention with h heads computes h parallel attention functions, each with separate parameters, then concatenates and linearly projects the results.
Given input matrices Q, K, V ∈Rn×dmodel: headi = Attention(QWQ i , KWK i , VWV i ) (4.1) MultiHead(Q, K, V) = Concat(head1, . . . , headh)WO (4.2) where: • WQ i , WK i ∈Rdmodel×dk: Query and key projections for head i • WV i ∈Rdmodel×dv: Value projection for head i • WO ∈Rhdv×dmodel: Output projection Each head i computes: headi = softmax QWQ i (KWK i )T √dk !
VWV i (4.3) The outputs from all heads, each of dimension n × dv, are concatenated along the feature dimension to produce a matrix of dimension n × (hdv), which is then linearly projected to dimension n × dmodel.
20 4.2. THEORETICAL ANALYSIS 21 4.1.2 Dimension Choice Typically, dimensions are chosen such that: dk = dv = dmodel h (4.4) With this choice, hdv = dmodel, and the total computational cost of multi-head attention is comparable to single-head attention with full dimensionality.
Example 4.1. For BERT base [11]: dmodel = 768, h = 12, so dk = dv = 64.
For GPT-3 [5]: dmodel = 12288, h = 96, so dk = dv = 128.
4.2 Theoretical Analysis 4.2.1 Representation Capacity Theorem 4.1 (Subspace Decomposition). Multi-head attention with h heads can be viewed as learning h different representation subspaces, where each head operates in a subspace of dimension dk = dmodel/h.
The projection matrices WQ i , WK i , WV i map the input into a lower-dimensional subspace where attention is computed. Different heads can learn to extract different features by focusing on different subspaces.
Theorem 4.2 (Expressiveness). Multi-head attention can represent a broader class of functions than single-head attention. Specifically, multi-head attention can simultaneously encode multiple relationships between positions, whereas single-head attention is limited to encoding a single weighted aggregation pattern.
Proof Sketch. Consider a single-head attention mechanism. The output at position i is: yi = n X j=1 αijvj (4.5) where αij is a scalar weight. The output is constrained to lie in the span of the value vectors {vj}.
With multi-head attention, the output is: yi = h X k=1 WO k   n X j=1 α(k) ij v(k) j   (4.6) where α(k) ij and v(k) j are the attention weights and values for head k. The output is now a weighted combination of outputs from different subspaces, allowing more flexible representations.
Each head can learn different attention patterns {α(k) ij }i,j. For instance: • Head 1 might learn to attend to adjacent positions (local context) • Head 2 might learn to attend to syntactically related positions • Head 3 might learn to attend to semantically similar positions The final output projection WO learns how to combine these different types of information.
4.2.2 Computational Complexity Proposition 4.3 (Complexity of Multi-Head Attention). For input sequence length n, model dimension dmodel, and h heads with dk = dv = dmodel/h, multi-head attention has complexity: O(n2dmodel + nd2 model) (4.7) which is the same as single-head attention with full dimensionality.
Proof. For each head i: 1. Computing QWQ i , KWK i , VWV i : 3 × O(ndmodeldk) = O(nd2 model/h) 2. Computing attention: O(n2dk) = O(n2dmodel/h) 22 CHAPTER 4. MULTI-HEAD ATTENTION For all h heads: h ×  O nd2 model h  + O n2dmodel h  = O(nd2 model + n2dmodel) (4.8) The final output projection Concat(. . .)WO costs O(nd2 model).
Total: O(n2dmodel + nd2 model), same as single-head attention.
This result shows that multi-head attention increases model capacity without increasing computational cost, provided dimensions are scaled appropriately.
4.3 Implementation and Optimization 4.3.1 Efficient Implementation Multi-head attention can be implemented efficiently using reshaped matrix operations that allow parallel computation of all heads.
Algorithm 1 Efficient Multi-Head Attention Require: Inputs Q, K, V ∈Rn×dmodel, heads h, dimensions dk = dv = dmodel/h Ensure: Output Y ∈Rn×dmodel 1: Compute combined projections: 2: Q′ ←QWQ where WQ ∈Rdmodel×dmodel 3: K′ ←KWK where WK ∈Rdmodel×dmodel 4: V′ ←VWV where WV ∈Rdmodel×dmodel 5: Reshape for heads: 6: Q′ ←reshape(Q′, [n, h, dk]) 7: K′ ←reshape(K′, [n, h, dk]) 8: V′ ←reshape(V′, [n, h, dv]) 9: Transpose to [h, n, dk] for parallel head computation 10: Compute attention for all heads in parallel: 11: S ←Q′K′T √dk ▷Shape: [h, n, n] 12: A ←softmax(S) ▷Row-wise for each head 13: O ←AV′ ▷Shape: [h, n, dv] 14: Transpose back to [n, h, dv] and reshape to [n, hdv] 15: Apply output projection: 16: Y ←OWO 17: return Y This implementation uses a single large matrix multiplication for all head projections (steps 1-3), rather than h separate multiplications. The reshaping operations (steps 4-6) are typically just view operations that don’t copy data.
The parallel computation of attention for all heads (steps 7-9) exploits the batch dimension of tensor operations, allowing modern GPUs to compute all heads simultaneously.
4.3.2 Memory Optimization For each head, we must store the attention weight matrix Ai ∈Rn×n during training for backpropagation.
With h heads, total attention memory is: Mattn = h × n2 × bytes per element (4.9) For long sequences, this becomes prohibitive. Several optimization strategies exist: Gradient Checkpointing: Don’t store attention weights during forward pass. Recompute them during the backward pass from saved inputs. Trades computation for memory.
Memory-Efficient Attention: Algorithms that compute attention gradients without materializing the full n × n matrices. These fuse softmax and matrix multiplication operations.
Sparse Attention: Restrict each position to attend to only a subset of positions, reducing the attention matrix from O(n2) to O(n√n) or O(n log n) depending on the pattern.
4.4. EMPIRICAL OBSERVATIONS 23 4.4 Empirical Observations 4.4.1 Head Specialization Empirical analysis of trained Transformer models reveals that different heads learn distinct attention pat-terns: Example 4.2 (Linguistic Phenomena in BERT). Analysis of BERT’s attention heads [9] shows: • Positional heads: Some heads attend primarily to the next or previous position, capturing sequential structure.
• Syntactic heads: Certain heads learn to follow dependency relationships, with attention patterns that resemble syntactic parse trees.
• Semantic heads: Some heads attend to semantically related words regardless of position, suggesting they capture semantic similarity.
• Delimiter heads: Heads that attend to special tokens like [SEP] and [CLS], using them as information hubs.
4.4.2 Layer-Wise Patterns The role of multi-head attention changes across layers: Early Layers: Tend to focus on local, surface-level features. Attention patterns are often local (high weights near the diagonal) or positional.
Middle Layers: Develop more complex patterns, including syntactic relationships and longer-range dependencies.
Late Layers: Often exhibit more diffuse attention patterns, aggregating information from many posi-tions. Some heads develop task-specific patterns related to the final prediction objective.
4.4.3 Head Importance Not all heads contribute equally. Pruning experiments show: • Many heads can be removed with minimal performance degradation • Some heads are critical, and their removal significantly hurts performance • Head importance varies by task and domain This redundancy suggests that multi-head attention may be over-parameterized, motivating research into more efficient variants.
Chapter 5 The Transformer Architecture The Transformer architecture [31] represents a complete neural network built entirely from attention mech-anisms, eliminating recurrence and convolution. This chapter provides a comprehensive treatment of all components, with complete mathematical derivations.
5.1 Architecture Overview The Transformer follows an encoder-decoder structure. Both encoder and decoder consist of stacked layers, each containing multi-head attention mechanisms and position-wise feed-forward networks, with residual connections and layer normalization.
5.1.1 Encoder Architecture The encoder processes the input sequence and produces a sequence of contextual representations. It consists of N identical layers (typically N = 6 or N = 12).
Each encoder layer contains two sub-layers: 1. Multi-head self-attention mechanism: Allows each position to attend to all positions in the encoder’s previous layer.
2. Position-wise fully connected feed-forward network: Applies the same feed-forward network in-dependently to each position.
Each sub-layer employs a residual connection followed by layer normalization: Output = LayerNorm(x + Sublayer(x)) (5.1) where Sublayer(x) is the function implemented by the sub-layer (either attention or feed-forward net-work).
5.1.2 Decoder Architecture The decoder generates the output sequence one element at a time, conditioned on previously generated elements and the encoder outputs. It also consists of N identical layers.
Each decoder layer contains three sub-layers: 1. Masked multi-head self-attention: Self-attention over the decoder’s previous layer, with masking to prevent positions from attending to subsequent positions.
2. Multi-head encoder-decoder attention: Queries come from the previous decoder layer, while keys and values come from the encoder output.
3. Position-wise feed-forward network: Same as in the encoder.
Like the encoder, each sub-layer employs residual connections and layer normalization. The complete architecture is shown in Figure 5.1.
5.2 Positional Encoding Since self-attention is permutation-equivariant, the Transformer has no inherent notion of sequence order.
To incorporate positional information, the Transformer adds positional encodings to the input embeddings.
24 5.2. POSITIONAL ENCODING 25 Encoder Input Embedding + Positional Encoding Encoder Layer ×N Multi-Head Self-Attention Add & Norm Feed Forward Add & Norm Encoder Output Decoder Output Embedding + Positional Encoding Decoder Layer ×N Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention Add & Norm Multi-Head Cross-Attention Add & Norm Feed Forward Add & Norm K, V Q Linear Softmax Output Probabilities Figure 5.1: Complete Transformer encoder-decoder architecture. Orange dashed lines represent residual connections. The encoder processes the input sequence, and the decoder generates the output sequence conditioned on encoder outputs (as Keys and Values) and previous decoder outputs (as Query in cross-attention).
5.2.1 Sinusoidal Positional Encoding The original Transformer uses sinusoidal functions of different frequencies: Definition 5.1 (Sinusoidal Positional Encoding). For position pos ∈{0, 1, . . . , n −1} and dimension i ∈ {0, 1, . . . , dmodel −1}: PE(pos,2i) = sin  pos 100002i/dmodel  (5.2) PE(pos,2i+1) = cos  pos 100002i/dmodel  (5.3) where even dimensions use sine and odd dimensions use cosine.
The wavelength of each dimension forms a geometric progression from 2π to 10000 · 2π.
5.2.2 Properties of Sinusoidal Encodings Proposition 5.1 (Relative Position Representation). For any fixed offset k, the positional encoding at position pos + k can be represented as a linear function of the positional encoding at position pos.
26 CHAPTER 5. THE TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE Proof. Using trigonometric identities: sin(α + β) = sin α cos β + cos α sin β, (5.4) cos(α + β) = cos α cos β −sin α sin β.
(5.5) Let ωi = 1/100002i/dmodel. Then: PE(pos+k,2i) = sin(ωi(pos + k)) (5.6) = sin(ωipos) cos(ωik) + cos(ωipos) sin(ωik) (5.7) = PE(pos,2i) cos(ωik) + PE(pos,2i+1) sin(ωik).
(5.8) Similarly, for the cosine term. This shows that PEpos+k can be expressed as a linear combination of PEpos with coefficients depending only on k and i, not on pos.
This property allows the model to attend to relative positions by learning appropriate linear trans-formations. Alternative approaches that directly encode relative position information into the attention mechanism have also been explored [27].
Mathematical Insight The sinusoidal encoding provides a unique encoding for each position while maintaining useful math-ematical properties. The model can potentially learn to attend by relative position because PEpos+k is a linear function of PEpos.
5.2.3 Learned Positional Embeddings An alternative to sinusoidal encodings is to learn positional embeddings as parameters: PEpos = Epos (5.9) where E ∈Rnmax×dmodel is a learned embedding matrix and nmax is the maximum sequence length.
Advantages: • Can potentially adapt better to specific tasks • No assumptions about the form of positional information Disadvantages: • Cannot extrapolate to sequences longer than seen during training • Requires learning O(nmaxdmodel) additional parameters Empirical results show that learned and sinusoidal positional encodings achieve similar performance, though sinusoidal encodings have the theoretical advantage of being able to extrapolate to longer sequences.
5.3 Feed-Forward Networks Each layer includes a position-wise fully connected feed-forward network that is applied identically and independently to each position.
Definition 5.2 (Position-Wise Feed-Forward Network). The feed-forward network consists of two linear transformations with a ReLU activation: FFN(x) = max(0, xW1 + b1)W2 + b2 (5.10) where: • W1 ∈Rdmodel×dff : First layer weights • b1 ∈Rdff : First layer bias • W2 ∈Rdff ×dmodel: Second layer weights 5.4. LAYER NORMALIZATION 27 • b2 ∈Rdmodel: Second layer bias Typically, dff = 4dmodel.
The feed-forward network is ”position-wise” because the same parameters are applied to each position independently. For an input sequence X ∈Rn×dmodel, the output is: [FFN(X)]i = FFN(xi) (5.11) where xi is row i of X.
5.3.1 Role and Interpretation The feed-forward network serves several purposes: Increased Capacity: The expansion to dff = 4dmodel dimensions provides additional representational capacity. The first layer projects to a higher dimension, and the second projects back.
Non-Linearity: The ReLU activation introduces non-linearity, allowing the network to learn complex functions. Without this, stacking linear layers would be equivalent to a single linear layer.
Position-Specific Processing: While attention aggregates information across positions, the feed-forward network processes each position independently, allowing position-specific transformations.
5.3.2 Alternative Activations While ReLU is standard, other activations have been explored: GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit): GELU(x) = xΦ(x) (5.12) where Φ is the cumulative distribution function of the standard normal distribution. GELU is used in BERT and GPT models.
Swish: Swish(x) = x · σ(βx) (5.13) where σ is the sigmoid function and β is a learned or fixed parameter.
These smooth activations can provide better gradient flow compared to ReLU.
5.4 Layer Normalization Layer normalization [2] normalizes activations across the feature dimension for each sample independently.
Definition 5.3 (Layer Normalization). For input x ∈Rd, layer normalization computes: µ = 1 d d X i=1 xi (5.14) σ2 = 1 d d X i=1 (xi −µ)2 (5.15) ˆ xi = xi −µ √ σ2 + ϵ (5.16) LayerNorm(x)i = γˆ xi + β (5.17) where ϵ is a small constant (e.g., 10−6) for numerical stability, and γ, β ∈Rd are learned parameters that allow the network to scale and shift the normalized values.
5.4.1 Properties and Benefits Normalization Across Features: Unlike batch normalization, which normalizes across the batch dimen-sion, layer normalization normalizes across features for each sample independently. This makes it suitable for variable-length sequences where batch statistics may be unreliable.
Training Stabilization: Layer normalization reduces internal covariate shift, stabilizing the distribu-tion of layer inputs and enabling faster training with higher learning rates.
Gradient Flow: By normalizing activations, layer normalization helps maintain gradients within a reasonable range, facilitating the training of deep networks.
28 CHAPTER 5. THE TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE 5.4.2 Placement: Pre-Norm vs Post-Norm Two variants exist for placing layer normalization relative to residual connections: Post-Norm (Original Transformer): y = LayerNorm(x + Sublayer(x)) (5.18) Pre-Norm: y = x + Sublayer(LayerNorm(x)) (5.19) Key Points Pre-norm has become more common in recent large models (e.g., GPT-3 [5], PaLM [8]) because it: • Facilitates training of very deep networks • Reduces gradient magnitudes, improving stability • Often converges faster in practice However, post-norm can achieve slightly better final performance with careful tuning.
5.5 Residual Connections Residual connections [17], introduced for computer vision, are crucial for training deep Transformers.
5.5.1 Formulation Each sub-layer in the Transformer employs a residual connection: y = x + Sublayer(x) (5.20) where Sublayer(x) is either attention or feed-forward computation.
5.5.2 Gradient Flow Analysis Theorem 5.2 (Residual Gradient Flow). Residual connections provide a direct path for gradients to flow from output to input, mitigating vanishing gradient problems in deep networks.
Proof. Consider a layer with input x and output y = x + F(x) where F is the sub-layer function.
During backpropagation, the gradient with respect to x is: ∂L ∂x = ∂L ∂y ∂y ∂x = ∂L ∂y  I + ∂F(x) ∂x  (5.21) where I is the identity matrix. The identity term ensures that gradients can flow directly without being attenuated by the sub-layer, even if ∂F (x) ∂x has small eigenvalues.
For a network with L layers, gradients flow as: ∂L ∂x0 = ∂L ∂xL L Y l=1  I + ∂Fl(xl−1) ∂xl−1  (5.22) The identity terms prevent the product from vanishing, as each factor is at least the identity.
This gradient flow property enables training of very deep Transformers (e.g., 96 layers in large language models) without vanishing gradients.
Chapter 6 Training and Optimization Training Transformer models requires careful attention to optimization algorithms, learning rate schedules, regularization techniques, and loss functions. This chapter provides a comprehensive treatment of these topics with mathematical analysis.
6.1 Loss Functions 6.1.1 Cross-Entropy Loss For sequence-to-sequence tasks, the Transformer is trained to maximize the likelihood of the target sequence given the source sequence.
Definition 6.1 (Sequence Cross-Entropy Loss). For a sequence y = (y1, . . . , ym) from vocabulary V of size |V |, the loss is: L(θ) = − m X t=1 log P(yt|y<t, x; θ) (6.1) where θ represents all model parameters, x is the source sequence, and y<t = (y1, . . . , yt−1) represents all previous target tokens.
The model outputs logits zt ∈R|V | at each position t, which are converted to probabilities via softmax: P(yt = v|y<t, x; θ) = exp(ztv) P|V | v′=1 exp(ztv′) (6.2) The loss for position t is then: Lt = −log P(yt|y<t, x; θ) = −zt,yt + log |V | X v=1 exp(ztv) (6.3) 6.1.2 Label Smoothing Label smoothing is a regularization technique that replaces hard targets with a mixture of the true label and a uniform distribution.
Definition 6.2 (Label Smoothing). Instead of using one-hot encoded targets, use smoothed targets: ˜ ytv = ( 1 −ϵ + ϵ |V | if v = yt ϵ |V | otherwise (6.4) where ϵ is the smoothing parameter (typically ϵ = 0.1).
The loss becomes: Lt = − |V | X v=1 ˜ ytv log P(yt = v|y<t, x; θ) (6.5) Proposition 6.1 (Effect of Label Smoothing). Label smoothing prevents the model from becoming over-confident in its predictions, encourages better calibration, and acts as a regularizer that can improve gener-alization.
The smoothed targets introduce entropy into the training signal, preventing the model from driving the logits of the correct class to +∞and all others to −∞, which can lead to overfitting.
29 30 CHAPTER 6. TRAINING AND OPTIMIZATION 6.2 Optimization Algorithm 6.2.1 Adam Optimizer The original Transformer uses the Adam optimizer [22], an adaptive learning rate method.
Algorithm 2 Adam Optimization Algorithm Require: Learning rate α, exponential decay rates β1, β2, small constant ϵ Require: Initial parameters θ0 1: Initialize first moment m0 ←0, second moment v0 ←0 2: Initialize time step t ←0 3: while not converged do 4: t ←t + 1 5: Compute gradient: gt ←∇θL(θt−1) 6: Update first moment: mt ←β1mt−1 + (1 −β1)gt 7: Update second moment: vt ←β2vt−1 + (1 −β2)g2 t 8: Compute bias-corrected moments: 9: ˆ mt ← mt 1−βt 1 10: ˆ vt ← vt 1−βt 2 11: Update parameters: θt ←θt−1 −α ˆ mt √ˆ vt+ϵ 12: end while Standard hyperparameters: β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.999, ϵ = 10−8.
Adam adapts the learning rate for each parameter based on estimates of first and second moments of the gradients, allowing different learning rates for different parameters.
6.2.2 Learning Rate Schedule The original Transformer employs a learning rate schedule that increases linearly during a warmup phase and then decreases.
Definition 6.3 (Warmup Learning Rate Schedule). The learning rate at step t is: lr(t) = d−0.5 model · min(t−0.5, t · warmup steps−1.5) (6.6) where warmup steps is the number of warmup steps (typically 4000-8000).
This schedule has two phases: 1. Warmup (t < warmup steps): Learning rate increases linearly: lr(t) = d−0.5 model · t · warmup steps−1.5 (6.7) 2. Decay (t ≥warmup steps): Learning rate decreases as t−0.5: lr(t) = d−0.5 model · t−0.5 (6.8) Mathematical Insight The warmup phase is crucial for stable training.
Without a warmup, Adam with a high initial learning rate can destabilize training, particularly in the first few iterations when second-moment estimates are inaccurate.
The d−0.5 model scaling ensures that the effective learning rate is adjusted based on model dimensionality, preventing excessively large updates in high-dimensional models.
6.3. REGULARIZATION TECHNIQUES 31 6.3 Regularization Techniques 6.3.1 Dropout Dropout [28] is applied at multiple points in the Transformer: Definition 6.4 (Dropout). During training, dropout randomly sets elements of the input to zero with probability p, and scales remaining elements by 1 1−p: Dropout(x)i = ( 0 with probability p xi 1−p with probability 1 −p (6.9) During evaluation, dropout is turned off: Dropout(x) = x.
Dropout is applied to: 1. Output of each sub-layer before the residual connection 2. Attention weights after softmax 3. Input embeddings Typical dropout rates: p = 0.1 for large datasets, p = 0.3 for smaller datasets.
6.3.2 Attention Dropout Attention dropout is applied to the attention weight matrix after softmax: y = Dropout(softmax(S))V (6.10) where S is the scaled score matrix. This prevents the model from relying too heavily on specific attention patterns.
6.3.3 Weight Decay Weight decay (L2 regularization) adds a penalty term to the loss: Ltotal = Ltask + λ 2 X i θ2 i (6.11) where λ is the weight decay coefficient (typically λ = 0.01 to 0.0001).
In Adam, weight decay is often decoupled from the adaptive learning rate (AdamW): θt ←θt−1 −α ˆ mt √ˆ vt + ϵ −αλθt−1 (6.12) 6.4 Gradient Computation and Backpropagation 6.4.1 Backpropagation Through Attention We derive the gradients for the attention mechanism to understand how errors propagate.
For attention output: yi = n X j=1 αijvj (6.13) Given gradient ∂L ∂yi from the next layer: Gradient with respect to values: ∂L ∂vj = m X i=1 αij ∂L ∂yi (6.14) Gradient with respect to attention weights: ∂L ∂αij = ∂L ∂yi T vj (6.15) 32 CHAPTER 6. TRAINING AND OPTIMIZATION Gradient through softmax: For the softmax function αij = exp(sij) P k exp(sik): ∂L ∂sij = n X k=1 ∂L ∂αik ∂αik ∂sij (6.16) The Jacobian of softmax is: ∂αik ∂sij = ( αij(1 −αij) if k = j −αijαik if k ̸= j (6.17) Therefore: ∂L ∂sij = αij ∂L ∂αij − n X k=1 αik ∂L ∂αik !
(6.18) These gradients are then backpropagated through the query and key computations.
6.4.2 Gradient Clipping To prevent exploding gradients, gradient clipping is often applied: g ← ( g if ∥g∥≤θ θ ∥g∥g if ∥g∥> θ (6.19) where θ is the clipping threshold (typically θ = 1.0 or 5.0).
6.5 Mixed Precision Training Modern Transformers are often trained using mixed precision, combining 16-bit and 32-bit floating point computations.
6.5.1 FP16/FP32 Mixed Precision Key Points Mixed precision training: • Stores model parameters in FP32 • Computes forward and backward passes in FP16 • Uses loss scaling to prevent underflow • Updates parameters in FP32 Benefits: • 2× memory savings for activations • 2-3× speedup on GPUs with Tensor Cores • Minimal impact on model quality with proper implementation Loss Scaling: Multiply loss by a large constant (e.g., 1024 or 2048) before backpropagation to keep gradients in FP16 range: Lscaled = L · scale (6.20) After computing gradients, divide by the scale factor before parameter updates: gtrue = gscaled scale (6.21) 6.6 Distributed Training Large Transformers require distributed training across multiple GPUs or machines.
6.6. DISTRIBUTED TRAINING 33 6.6.1 Data Parallelism Each GPU maintains a complete copy of the model and processes different batches. Gradients are averaged across GPUs before parameter updates.
Algorithm: 1. Partition batch across GPUs 2. Each GPU computes the forward pass on its partition 3. Each GPU computes gradients via backpropagation 4. All-reduce operation averages gradients across GPUs 5. Each GPU updates its model parameters 6.6.2 Model Parallelism For models too large to fit on a single GPU, different layers or attention heads are placed on different GPUs.
Tensor Parallelism: Splits individual tensors (e.g., weight matrices) across GPUs, requiring commu-nication during forward and backward passes.
Pipeline Parallelism: Assigns different layers to different GPUs, processing micro-batches in a pipelined fashion to reduce idle time.
Chapter 7 Conclusion and Future Directions This comprehensive treatment has established the mathematical foundations of attention mechanisms and their implementation in the Transformer architecture. From the fundamental attention operation through multi-head attention, positional encoding, and training dynamics, we have developed a rigorous understand-ing of these powerful sequence modeling tools. Attention mechanisms represent a fundamental paradigm shift in how neural networks process sequential information, moving from fixed compression through recur-rent states to flexible, learned aggregation patterns. The success of Transformers across natural language processing, computer vision, and multimodal domains demonstrates the broad applicability of this approach.
7.1 Achievements and Impact The Transformer architecture has fundamentally reshaped modern machine learning. Large language models built on Transformer foundations, including GPT-3 [5], PaLM [8], and LLaMA [30], have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across diverse tasks through in-context learning and few-shot adaptation. These models have achieved performance levels that approach or exceed human capabilities on many benchmarks, while also revealing emergent properties not present in smaller-scale systems. Beyond language processing, attention mechanisms have enabled breakthroughs in protein structure prediction through AlphaFold [20], revolutionized computer vision with Vision Transformers [12], and facilitated multimodal understanding through models like CLIP [24] and Flamingo [1]. The versatility of attention as a general-purpose mechanism for relationship modeling has proven applicable across scientific domains, from molecular dynamics to climate modeling.
7.2 Efficiency and Scalability Challenges Despite these successes, the quadratic complexity of standard attention with respect to sequence length remains a fundamental limitation. For sequences of length n, computing all pairwise attention scores re-quires O(n2) operations and memory, making it computationally prohibitive for very long contexts. This has motivated extensive research into efficient attention variants. Sparse attention patterns, as explored in Sparse Transformers [6] and Longformer [4], reduce complexity by restricting attention to local windows and selected global positions. These methods achieve linear or near-linear complexity while maintaining performance on tasks requiring long-range dependencies. Big Bird [34] combines local, global, and ran-dom attention patterns, proving theoretically that sparse attention can approximate full attention while dramatically reducing computational cost.
Low-rank approximation methods offer another approach to efficiency. Linformer [32] projects keys and values to lower dimensions, reducing complexity to O(nk) where k ≪n. Linear attention methods [21] and the Performer [7] use random feature approximations to estimate attention scores, achieving linear complex-ity through kernel methods. These approaches trade some expressiveness for computational efficiency, with empirical results suggesting the trade-off is often favorable. Recent work on Flash Attention [10] demon-strates that algorithmic optimizations exploiting hardware characteristics can yield substantial speedups without sacrificing accuracy. By carefully managing memory access patterns and leveraging GPU memory hierarchy, Flash Attention reduces memory reads/writes while computing exact attention, achieving 2-4x speedups over standard implementations. This highlights that implementation details matter as much as algorithmic complexity for practical performance.
7.3 Theoretical Understanding While the empirical success of Transformers is undeniable, the theoretical understanding of why and how they work remains incomplete. Recent theoretical work has begun addressing fundamental questions about Transformers’ representational capacity and learning dynamics. Yun et al. [33] proved that Transformers 34 7.4. INTERPRETABILITY AND ANALYSIS 35 with sufficient depth can represent any continuous sequence-to-sequence function, establishing universal ap-proximation properties. However, these results require impractical depth, leaving open questions about what functions Transformers can efficiently learn in practice. Analysis of attention as performing similarity-based retrieval [13] provides intuition for how Transformers process information, but a rigorous characterization of learned representations remains challenging. Understanding training dynamics presents additional chal-lenges. The interplay between attention layers, feed-forward networks, and residual connections creates complex optimization landscapes. Recent work on neural tangent kernels for Transformers [18] provides some theoretical grounding, but the gap between infinite-width theory and finite practical models limits applicability. Developing a theory that explains phenomena like in-context learning, where models adapt to new tasks from examples in their input without gradient updates, represents an important frontier.
7.4 Interpretability and Analysis The attention weights in Transformers provide some interpretability, allowing inspection of which input positions influence each output. However, attention patterns often prove difficult to interpret meaningfully, particularly in deeper layers where representations become increasingly abstract. Research into Transformer interpretability has revealed both insights and limitations. Studies of attention patterns in trained mod-els [9] have identified specialized attention heads that attend to specific syntactic relationships in language tasks, suggesting some degree of interpretable structure. However, other work [19] demonstrates that at-tention weights do not always correspond to model behavior or feature importance, cautioning against over-interpreting attention as explanation. The distinction between attention as a mechanism and attention as explanation remains an active research question. Advances in mechanistic interpretability [13] aim to reverse-engineer learned algorithms in Transformers by analyzing internal representations and information flow. These approaches have successfully identified interpretable circuits for specific tasks, but scaling to large models trained on diverse data remains challenging. Developing robust methods for understanding, debugging, and controlling Transformer behavior represents a critical research direction for deploying these systems safely and reliably.
7.5 Future Directions 7.5.1 Long-Context Modeling Extending Transformers to handle extremely long contexts efficiently remains an important goal. Applica-tions including book-length document understanding, long-form dialogue, and scientific literature analysis require processing sequences far exceeding current practical limits.
Approaches combining hierarchical processing, memory mechanisms, and retrieval augmentation show promise for scaling to arbitrarily long contexts while maintaining computational tractability.
7.5.2 Multimodal Integration While attention has proven effective for multimodal learning, better methods for integrating diverse data types are needed. Cross-modal attention mechanisms that capture fine-grained alignments between vision and language [1] represent progress, but open questions remain about optimal architectures for three or more modalities, temporal alignment across modalities, and handling modality-specific structure.
7.5.3 Efficient Architectures Hybrid architectures combining Transformers with other mechanisms may offer better efficiency-performance trade-offs.
State space models like S4 [15] achieve competitive performance with linear complexity by replacing attention with structured linear operators.
Understanding when attention is necessary versus when alternatives suffice could enable more efficient architectures tailored to specific tasks. Mixture-of-Experts approaches [14] offer another path to scaling by activating only subsets of model parameters for each input. Combining sparse attention with sparse expert routing may enable models with trillions of parameters while maintaining manageable computational costs.
7.5.4 Continual and Few-Shot Learning While Transformers exhibit impressive in-context learning, improving their ability to efficiently acquire and retain new knowledge remains important. Better understanding and enhancing few-shot learning capabili-ties could reduce data requirements for adapting models to specialized domains. Developing architectures 36 CHAPTER 7. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS that support continual learning without catastrophic forgetting while maintaining computational efficiency represents a key challenge.
7.5.5 Environmental Considerations The computational cost of training large Transformers raises important questions about energy consump-tion and environmental impact. Research into more efficient training methods, better transfer learning to reduce training from scratch, and specialized hardware for Transformer inference addresses these concerns.
Developing metrics and best practices for sustainable AI development will become increasingly important as models scale.
7.6 Concluding Remarks Attention mechanisms have transformed artificial intelligence by providing a flexible, powerful framework for modeling relationships in sequential and structured data.
The mathematical foundations developed in this monograph establish the theoretical basis for understanding these mechanisms, while highlighting open questions and challenges. As research addresses efficiency, theoretical understanding, interpretability, and domain-specific applications, attention-based architectures will continue evolving. Whether through improved Transformer variants, hybrid approaches combining multiple mechanisms, or entirely new archi-tectures inspired by attention principles, the core insight of learned, adaptive aggregation will remain central to progress in machine learning. The next decade promises continued innovation, building on the attention paradigm established over the past several years.

title: Attention and Memory in Deep Learning and NLP
content:
# Attention and Memory in Deep Learning and NLP

A recent trend in Deep Learning are Attention Mechanisms. In an [interview](https://re-work.co/blog/deep-learning-ilya-sutskever-google-openai), Ilya Sutskever, now the research director of OpenAI, mentioned that Attention Mechanisms are one of the most exciting advancements, and that they are here to stay. That sounds exciting. But what are Attention Mechanisms?

Attention Mechanisms in Neural Networks are (very) loosely based on the visual attention mechanism found in humans. Human visual attention is well-studied and while there exist different models, all of them essentially come down to being able to focus on a certain region of an image with “high resolution” while perceiving the surrounding image in “low resolution”, and then adjusting the focal point over time.

Attention in Neural Networks has a long history, particularly in image recognition. Examples include [Learning to combine foveal glimpses with a third-order Boltzmann machine](http://papers.nips.cc/paper/4089-learning-to-combine-foveal-glimpses-with-a-third-order-boltzmann-machine) or [Learning where to Attend with Deep Architectures for Image Tracking](http://arxiv.org/abs/1109.3737). But only recently have attention mechanisms made their way into recurrent neural networks architectures that are typically used in NLP (and increasingly also in vision). That’s what we’ll focus on in this post.

#### What problem does Attention solve? [#](#what-problem-does-attention-solve)

To understand what attention can do for us, let’s use Neural Machine Translation (NMT) as an example. Traditional Machine Translation systems typically rely on sophisticated feature engineering based on the statistical properties of text. In short, these systems are complex, and a lot of engineering effort goes into building them. Neural Machine Translation systems work a bit differently. In NMT, we map the meaning of a sentence into a fixed-length vector representation and then generate a translation based on that vector. By not relying on things like n-gram counts and instead trying to capture the higher-level meaning of a text, NMT systems generalize to new sentences better than many other approaches. Perhaps more importantly, NTM systems are much easier to build and train, and they don’t require any manual feature engineering. In fact, [a simple implementation in Tensorflow](https://www.tensorflow.org/versions/master/tutorials/seq2seq/index.html#sequence-to-sequence-models) is no more than a few hundred lines of code.

Most NMT systems work by *encoding* the source sentence (e.g. a German sentence) into a vector using a [Recurrent Neural Network](https://dennybritz.com/posts/wildml/recurrent-neural-networks-tutorial-part-1/), and then *decoding* an English sentence based on that vector, also using a RNN.

In the picture above, “Echt”, “Dicke” and “Kiste” words are fed into an encoder, and after a special signal (not shown) the decoder starts producing a translated sentence. The decoder keeps generating words until a special end of sentence token is produced. Here, the \(h\) vectors represent the internal state of the encoder.

If you look closely, you can see that the decoder is supposed to generate a translation solely based on the last hidden state \(h\_3\) from the encoder. This \(h\_3\) vector must encode everything we need to know about the source sentence. It must fully capture its meaning. In more technical terms, that vector is a sentence *embedding*. In fact, if you plot the embeddings of different sentences in a low dimensional space using PCA or t-SNE for dimensionality reduction, [you can see](http://arxiv.org/abs/1409.3215) that semantically similar phrases end up close to each other. That’s pretty amazing.

Still, it seems somewhat unreasonable to assume that we can encode all information about a potentially very long sentence into a single vector and then have the decoder produce a good translation based on only that. Let’s say your source sentence is 50 words long. The first word of the English translation is probably highly correlated with the first word of the source sentence. But that means decoder has to consider information from 50 steps ago, and that information needs to be somehow encoded in the vector. Recurrent Neural Networks are known to have problems dealing with such long-range dependencies. In theory, architectures like [LSTMs](https://dennybritz.com/posts/wildml/recurrent-neural-networks-tutorial-part-4/) should be able to deal with this, but in practice long-range dependencies are still problematic. For example, researchers have found that reversing the source sequence (feeding it backwards into the encoder) produces significantly better results because it shortens the path from the decoder to the relevant parts of the encoder. Similarly, [feeding an input sequence twice](http://arxiv.org/abs/1410.4615) also seems to help a network to better memorize things.

I consider the approach of reversing a sentence a “hack”. It makes things work better in practice, but it’s not a principled solution. Most translation benchmarks are done on languages like French and German, which are quite similar to English (even Chinese word order is quite similar to English). But there are languages (like Japanese) where the last word of a sentence could be highly predictive of the first word in an English translation. In that case, reversing the input would make things worse. So, what’s an alternative? Attention Mechanisms.

With an attention mechanism we no longer try encode the full source sentence into a fixed-length vector. Rather, we allow the decoder to “attend” to different parts of the source sentence at each step of the output generation. Importantly, we let the model **learn** what to attend to based on the input sentence and what it has produced so far. So, in languages that are pretty well aligned (like English and German) the decoder would probably choose to attend to things sequentially. Attending to the first word when producing the first English word, and so on. That’s what was done in [Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate](http://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473) and look as follows:

Here, The \(y\)‘s are our translated words produced by the decoder, and the \(x\)‘s are our source sentence words. The above illustration uses a bidirectional recurrent network, but that’s not important and you can just ignore the inverse direction. The important part is that each decoder output word \(y\_t\) now depends on a **weighted combination of all the input states**, not just the last state. The \(a\)‘s are weights that define in how much of each input state should be considered for each output. So, if \(a\_{3,2}\) is a large number, this would mean that the decoder pays a lot of attention to the second state in the source sentence while producing the third word of the target sentence. The \(a’s\) are typically normalized to sum to 1 (so they are a distribution over the input states).

A big advantage of attention is that it gives us the ability to interpret and visualize what the model is doing. For example, by visualizing the attention weight matrix \(a\) when a sentence is translated, we can understand how the model is translating:

Here we see that while translating from French to English, the network attends sequentially to each input state, but sometimes it attends to two words at time while producing an output, as in translation “la Syrie” to “Syria” for example.

#### The Cost of Attention [#](#the-cost-of-attention)

If we look a bit more look closely at the equation for attention we can see that attention comes at a cost. We need to calculate an attention value for each combination of input and output word. If you have a 50-word input sequence and generate a 50-word output sequence that would be 2500 attention values. That’s not too bad, but if you do character-level computations and deal with sequences consisting of hundreds of tokens the above attention mechanisms can become prohibitively expensive.

Actually, that’s quite counterintuitive. Human attention is something that’s supposed to **save** computational resources. By focusing on one thing, we can neglect many other things. But that’s not really what we’re doing in the above model. We’re essentially looking at everything in detail before deciding what to focus on. Intuitively that’s equivalent outputting a translated word, and then going back through *all* of your internal memory of the text in order to decide which word to produce next. That seems like a waste, and not at all what humans are doing. In fact, it’s more akin to memory access, not attention, which in my opinion is somewhat of a misnomer (more on that below). Still, that hasn’t stopped attention mechanisms from becoming quite popular and performing well on many tasks.

An alternative approach to attention is to use Reinforcement Learning to predict an approximate location to focus to. That sounds a lot more like human attention, and that’s what’s done in [Recurrent Models of Visual Attention](http://arxiv.org/abs/1406.6247).

#### Attention beyond Machine Translation [#](#attention-beyond-machine-translation)

So far we’ve looked at attention applied to Machine Translation. But the same attention mechanism from above can be applied to any recurrent model. So let’s look at a few more examples.

In [Show, Attend and Tell](http://arxiv.org/abs/1502.03044) the authors apply attention mechanisms to the problem of generating image descriptions. They use a Convolutional Neural Network to “encode” the image, and a Recurrent Neural Network with attention mechanisms to generate a description. By visualizing the attention weights (just like in the translation example), we interpret what the model is looking at while generating a word:

In [Grammar as a Foreign Language](http://arxiv.org/abs/1412.7449), the authors use a Recurrent Neural Network with attention mechanisk to generate sentence parse trees. The visualized attention matrix gives insight into how the network generates those trees:

In [Teaching Machines to Read and Comprehend](http://arxiv.org/abs/1506.03340), the authors use a RNN to read a text, read a (synthetically generated) question, and then produce an answer. By visualizing the attention matrix we can see where the networks “looks” while it tries to find the answer to the question:

#### Attention = (Fuzzy) Memory? [#](#attention--fuzzy-memory)

The basic problem that the attention mechanism solves is that it allows the network to refer back to the input sequence, instead of forcing it to encode all information into one fixed-length vector. As I mentioned above, I think that attention is somewhat of a misnomer. Interpreted another way, the attention mechanism is simply giving the network access to its internal memory, which is the hidden state of the encoder. In this interpretation, instead of choosing what to “attend” to, the network chooses what to retrieve from memory. Unlike typical memory, the memory access mechanism here is soft, which means that the network retrieves a weighted combination of all memory locations, not a value from a single discrete location. Making the memory access soft has the benefit that we can easily train the network end-to-end using backpropagation (though there have been non-fuzzy approaches where the gradients are calculated using sampling methods instead of backpropagation).

Memory Mechanisms themselves have a much longer history. The hidden state of a standard Recurrent Neural Network is itself a type of internal memory. RNNs suffer from the [vanishing gradient problem](%28https://dennybritz.com/posts/wildml/recurrent-neural-networks-tutorial-part-3/%29) that prevents them from learning long-range dependencies. [LSTMs](http://deeplearning.cs.cmu.edu/pdfs/Hochreiter97_lstm.pdf) improved upon this by using a gating mechanism that allows for explicit memory deletes and updates.

The trend towards more complex memory structures is now continuing. [End-to-End Memory Networks](http://arxiv.org/abs/1503.08895) allow the network to read same input sequence multiple times before making an output, updating the memory contents at each step. For example, answering a question by making multiple reasoning steps over an input story. However, when the networks parameter weights are tied in a certain way, the memory mechanism inEnd-to-End Memory Networks identical to the attention mechanism presented here, only that it makes multiple hops over the memory (because it tries to integrate information from multiple sentences).

[Neural Turing Machines](https://github.com/dennybritz/deeplearning-papernotes/blob/master/neural-turing-machines.md) use a similar form of memory mechanism, but with a more sophisticated type of addressing that using both content-based (like here) and location-based addressing, allowing the network to learn addressing pattern to execute simple computer programs, like sorting algorithms.

It’s likely that in the future we will see a clearer distinction between memory and attention mechanisms, perhaps along the lines of [Reinforcement Learning Neural Turing Machines](http://arxiv.org/abs/1505.00521), which try to learn access patterns to deal with external interfaces.

title: Attention Mechanisms in Deep Learning - Graph AI
content:
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/669a7557dd27627eb2501a29/66c6573f944a1d2d2a52476b_tyler_davis_headshot_launchnotes.avif)

Tyler Davis

May 27, 2025

# Attention Mechanisms in Deep Learning: Beyond Transformers Explained

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/plugins/Basic/assets/placeholder.60f9b1840c.svg)

In the era of artificial intelligence and deep learning, [attention mechanisms](https://www.graphapp.ai/blog/chatgpt-technical-deep-dive-understanding-the-inner-workings) have emerged as a pivotal concept that allows models to focus on specific parts of the input data, yielding more effective outcomes than traditional methods. Initially popularized by Transformers, attention mechanisms can also be applied in various models, including recurrent and convolutional neural networks. In this article, we will explore the basics of attention mechanisms, delve deeper into Transformers, discuss alternatives, and consider the future of these techniques.

## Understanding the Basics of Attention Mechanisms

Attention mechanisms enable models to weigh the importance of different parts of the input data dynamically. Instead of processing the entire input uniformly, attention allows models to focus on crucial segments, thereby optimizing the learning process. This is particularly significant in tasks involving sequences, such as [natural language processing](https://www.graphapp.ai/blog/transfer-learning-in-nlp-leveraging-pre-trained-models-for-specific-tasks) (NLP) and image analysis. The ability to selectively concentrate on relevant portions of the input not only enhances the efficiency of the model but also allows for a more nuanced understanding of complex data structures.

For instance, in the realm of NLP, attention mechanisms can help disambiguate words that have multiple meanings based on their context. By focusing on surrounding words, the model can better grasp the intended meaning, leading to more accurate interpretations. Similarly, in image analysis, attention mechanisms can highlight specific areas of an image that are more informative, such as the face in a crowd, thereby improving the model's ability to recognize and classify objects.

### The Role of Attention Mechanisms in Deep Learning

Attention mechanisms play a transformative role in the way deep learning models analyze and interpret data. By facilitating selective focus, these mechanisms help mitigate the issues related to information overload. For example, when translating text from one language to another, an attention mechanism allows the model to concentrate on relevant words in the source language that correspond to the target language, improving translation accuracy. This selective attention is akin to how humans process language, where we instinctively focus on key phrases that carry the most meaning.

Moreover, attention mechanisms enable models to maintain contextual awareness. They dynamically adjust which parts of the input to prioritize based on the task at hand, leading to enhanced performance across various applications, such as summarization, sentiment analysis, and image captioning. In summarization tasks, for instance, attention can help identify the most salient sentences in a document, ensuring that the generated summary captures the essence of the original text. This adaptability is what makes attention mechanisms a cornerstone of modern deep learning architectures.

### Key Components of Attention Mechanisms

There are several vital components that constitute an attention mechanism. The fundamental elements include:

Understanding these components is crucial for implementing broader variations of attention mechanisms that build on the basic ideas, like multi-head attention, which enhances performance by introducing multiple sets of query-key-value groups. This allows the model to capture different types of relationships within the data simultaneously. For example, in a sentence, one head may focus on syntactic relationships, while another may capture semantic meanings, leading to a richer representation of the input. Such innovations have propelled the development of state-of-the-art models, including Transformers, which have revolutionized the field of deep learning and set new benchmarks across various tasks.

## Diving Deeper into Transformers

Transformers, introduced in the landmark paper "Attention is All You Need," revolutionized NLP by leveraging attention mechanisms in a unique way. Unlike their predecessors, which relied heavily on recurrent connections, Transformers are grounded in an entirely attention-based architecture.

### The Architecture of Transformers

At the heart of the Transformer architecture lies the encoder-decoder structure. Each encoder consists of multiple layers that apply self-attention mechanisms to capture relationships within the input data. The decoder, on the other hand, also applies self-attention and incorporates outputs from the encoder to generate predictions.

This architecture allows for parallel processing, significantly improving training times and scalability compared to traditional methods. Furthermore, the positional encoding added to the inputs enables the model to retain information about the position of words, something essential for understanding sequence order. The use of multi-head attention further enhances the model's ability to focus on different parts of the input simultaneously, allowing it to capture a richer set of relationships and nuances within the data.

### The Limitations of Transformers

While Transformers have proven to be highly effective, they also come with limitations. One major drawback is their computational intensity. The self-attention mechanism scales quadratically with the sequence length, which can make them inefficient for very long sequences. This necessitates the development of optimization techniques and variations to handle larger datasets more effectively.

Additionally, Transformers require substantial memory resources, which can be a barrier for many deploying the technology in resource-constrained environments. These limitations prompt researchers to explore alternative architectures that incorporate attention mechanisms. For instance, models like Longformer and Reformer have been proposed to mitigate these issues by using sparse attention patterns or locality-sensitive hashing, respectively. These innovations aim to maintain the strengths of the Transformer architecture while addressing its computational challenges, thereby expanding its applicability across various domains and tasks.

## Exploring Alternatives to Transformers

As the landscape of AI continues to evolve, researchers have investigated other models where attention mechanisms can be effectively integrated. Notably, two significant alternatives are Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs).

### Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Attention

RNNs are designed to handle sequential data, making them a natural choice for tasks like speech recognition and time series analysis. However, traditional RNNs may struggle with long-term dependencies due to vanishing gradient problems. By incorporating attention mechanisms, RNNs can dynamically focus on important input sequences and [mitigate these challenges](https://www.graphapp.ai/blog/adversarial-machine-learning-strategies-for-developing-robust-ai-models). This enhancement allows RNNs to improve performance on complex tasks where retaining context over long sequences is critical. Furthermore, variations such as Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks and Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs) have been developed to address these limitations. These architectures utilize gating mechanisms to control the flow of information, effectively remembering or forgetting data as needed, which complements the attention mechanism and further enhances the model's ability to capture intricate patterns in sequential data.

### Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Attention

Initially developed for image processing, CNNs have proven their versatility in various fields. Recently, attention mechanisms have been integrated into CNNs to enhance their ability to focus on critical spatial features in the input data. This integration allows CNNs to prioritize relevant regions in images or localized data points in other types of input, leading to better performance in tasks such as object detection and image segmentation. The combination of CNNs with attention not only improves accuracy but also [provides interpretability](https://www.graphapp.ai/blog/explainable-ai-xai-techniques-for-transparent-machine-learning-models), as it becomes easier to visualize which parts of an image the model considers important. This has significant implications in fields like medical imaging, where understanding the rationale behind a model's decision can be as crucial as the decision itself. Moreover, advancements in techniques such as Spatial Attention and Channel Attention further refine the model's focus, allowing for a more nuanced understanding of the data being processed.

## The Future of Attention Mechanisms

As attention mechanisms continue to evolve, new trends and insights emerge that pave the way for future advancements in AI technology.

### Emerging Trends in Attention Mechanisms

Research in attention mechanisms is rapidly expanding, with studies focusing on hybrid models that combine the strengths of attention with other architectures, leading to [novel approaches](https://www.graphapp.ai/blog/llama-for-developers-a-comprehensive-guide) like Attention-based RNNs or CNNs. Additionally, more efficient attention algorithms are being explored to reduce computational costs while maintaining performance, which is paramount for real-world applications. Recent innovations include sparse attention mechanisms that selectively focus on a subset of inputs, dramatically improving processing speed and resource allocation. This shift towards efficiency not only enhances performance but also opens up possibilities for deploying complex models on edge devices, making advanced AI accessible in mobile and IoT environments.

### The Impact of Attention Mechanisms on AI Development

The proliferation of attention mechanisms is driving significant changes in AI development. They are becoming a standard part of the toolkit for machine learning practitioners, enabling them to tackle increasingly complex tasks. As models become more capable of understanding contextual relationships in data, we can expect improvements across a wide range of applications, from automated translation and conversation systems to image analysis and sentiment predictions. Furthermore, the integration of attention mechanisms into multi-modal learning frameworks allows for richer data interpretation, where models can simultaneously process and relate information from text, images, and audio. This capability is crucial for developing more sophisticated AI systems that can mimic human-like understanding and reasoning, thereby enhancing user interactions and experience.

## Conclusion: The Importance of Understanding Attention Mechanisms

In conclusion, a thorough understanding of attention mechanisms is essential for anyone involved in deep learning and AI research. As models evolve and new architectures emerge, attention mechanisms will maintain a central role in shaping the future of intelligent systems. By harnessing the power of attention, developers can unlock new potentials in their projects, leading to enhanced performance, more profound insights, and ultimately, more capable AI systems that enhance our understanding of the world.

# History of Attention Mechanism & an Introduction to Self-Attention with Visuals & code— Explained

[Shravan Kumar](/@shravankoninti?source=post_page---byline--a1529c79923e---------------------------------------)

14 min readMar 22, 2025

This is part 2 of the blog series — **“Build DeepSeek From Scratch”.**

You can find part 1 of the blog [here](/@shravankoninti/llm-architecture-journey-of-a-token-through-the-llm-architecture-f934bedf2984).

Today we are going to understand what is **Self Attention**, but before that we need to understand

* What is attention itself
* What’s the need for attention mechanism
* Why is it a game changer in this LLM space

> ***The need for an attention mechanism***

Here is how the field of generative AI evolved with some significant breakthroughs.

* 1966 : ELIZA chatbot
* 1980's : RNN
* 1997 : LSTMs
* 2014 : Attention + RNN
* 2017 : Attention + Transformer (Encoder + Decoder)
* 2018 : Attention + GPT (Decoder only)

In 1966, ELIZA was one of the first conversational chatbots developed. Here is how it looks. You can see below that for the questions I asked, it was NOT able to reply properly, but at present, if you ask the ChatGPT, it answers very well.

In 1980s — RNN (Recurrent Neural Network) and in 1997 — LSTMs (Long Short Term Memory) respectively, played a major role in the evolution of deep learning and laid the foundation for language modeling. But one of the issues with Neural Networks is they cannot deal with memory, and why we need this memory as important is because if you are predicting the next word and it is a task to do—then it requires the previous words and context around it.

Let me explain about what the **context** is for, e.g., if I give a corpus of text with ……..I am from Hyderabad, India \*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*. If I ask a question and ask, what is my native language, which I spoke? Then for this question, the contextual importance needs to be given to *my location (Hyderabad, India)* rest all the information is not important.

For a language translation task that comes under the sequence-to-sequence prediction task with RNN.

Each hidden state with h1, h2, and h3 will have the context of previous words. The hidden state h3 will act like a context bottleneck, which has concise input information (accumulated form h1, h2) that will be fed to the decoder block. The main problem with RNN is

Imagine that we have a paragraph like this :

*“I have embarked on an exciting journey into the realm of Generative AI, where I am learning how advanced machine learning techniques empower computers to create content that ranges from natural language to compelling visual art. Delving into this innovative field, I am studying various models — such as generative adversarial networks (GANs) and autoregressive models — that help machines generate creative outputs by understanding and replicating patterns found in vast datasets. As I navigate through the complexities of neural networks, deep learning frameworks, and algorithmic artistry, I am not only honing my technical skills but also gaining insight into how these models can revolutionize industries like entertainment, design, and automated content creation. This learning experience is both challenging and inspiring, fueling my curiosity about the limitless possibilities that Generative AI holds for the future.”*

If we can read the above paragraph, and if the task was to translate — then how do we do that? It is impossible for us to translate each word by word, but what we might remember is some summary at a very high level. The decoder does not get the information from each word to word that you can find in the encoder block and what it gets is the final hidden state of the encoder which has a summarized information of all the hidden states, this is the biggest roadblock for this RNN.

> **Context bottleneck in RNNs is not good for retaining long range context.**

**What is the ideal solution?**

We should have mechanism in decoder for each word output , where it should be able to detect what is the weightage or importance that needs to be given for each hidden state, and all the hidden state outputs become input to the decoder with some varied importance weights. This is where we touch upon the very important concept called "Attention."

**We need to selectively access parts of the input sequence during decoding.**

Alpha 11 , Alpha21 and Alpha31 will be acting like importance weightages for S1.

We always feel like — attention is all you need is the paper which introduced the attention. But actually the attention mechanism has been introduced with “Bahdanau attention”. in the year 2016. This is where we have RNN + Attention mechanism introduced.

Please read the paper [here](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1409.0473).

Interestingly, only three years later, researchers found that RNN architectures are not required for building deep neural networks for natural language processing and proposed the original transformer architecture with a self-attention mechanism inspired by the Bahdanau attention mechanism.

**Self attention:**

* It is a mechanism that allows each position in the input sequence to attend to all positions in the same sequence.

Let us say we have the sentence : “The next day is bright”. For example if we are looking at the word “next”, we need to see how much attention is need to be given for all other words in the same sequence.

The context vector is more richer than the embedding vector as it contains whole information of the neighbouring words but the input embedding vector will contain only token embedding + positional embedding.

Hence the input embedding and context neigbours get augmented together then we get Context Vector. Hence context vector is an enriched embedding vector. It combines information from all other input elements.

In **self-attention**, context vectors play a crucial role. Their purpose is to create enriched representations of each element in an input sequence (like a sentence), incorporating information from all other elements in the sequence. **This is essential in LLMs, which need to understand the relationship and relevance of words in a sentence to each other.**

How do we go from the input embedding vector to context vector?

Each respective word is considered as “Query” and all other words are considered as “Keys” and lets do a dot product between each word with other words and their values are represented as alphas and they are called as attention scores.

The context vector for “next” will be:

Similarly, we can do the same for other words as well. This approach might not work well. The dot product actually encapsulates if both vectors are similar/closer to each other or not. We can calculate Alpha21, Alpha22, Alpha23, Alpha24 and Alpha25 by simple dot product.

The dot product by the vector 1 and 2 is higher than that of 1 and 3.

**This approach of calculations for context vector does NOT work... ….and what’s wrong with this approach then?**

Let me take an example to show this — for e.g., consider the sentence

**“The dog chased the ball, but it couldn’t catch it.”**

Consider that we want to calculate the attention for the word or query token **"it”.**

The dot product between “dog” and “it”, “ball” and “it” is calculated, and the respective values are 0.51 each. So it produces the ambiguity on which word or token is it giving high preference to when the query is “it” as both values are the same.

**Notice that both attention scores come out identical (0.51).**

A simple dot product cannot distinguish between subtle contextual relationships here. It doesn’t consider the context of “chased,” “couldn’t catch,” or linguistic nuance such as the fact that “catch” is more likely to refer to a moving object (the ball).

## Get Shravan Kumar’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

**The problem is that a simple dot product only measures basic semantic similarity, which isn’t sufficient to resolve more nuanced contextual ambiguities.**

## **What is the option 2:**

> Instead of directly using embeddings, we transform each embedding through trained matrices and train these matrices through back propagation.

We get these W*Q* and W*K* matrices to be trained by backpropagation, and initially it starts with some random values, and the neural network will do the job of updating values.

Now if we plot all these vectors with input embedding space to context vector space.

Now if we compute the scores of attention, it will look like this for both Query (“it”) and Keys (“ball”, “dog”). Attention scores are calculated by Query and Keys dot product.

We cannot figure out on the rule for attention mechanism ourselves; we cannot figure out how to calculate the attention scores hence we leave it to neural networks to handle them for us. Now having got these attention scores, let us focus on **Self Attention With Trainable Weights.**

## Self Attention Mechanism — Handwritten from scratch

Let us see a step-by-step procedure on how we exactly take this input embedding vector to context vectors. The focus will be more on how the trainable matrices are calculated ie., Q, K, V.

Let us assume we have a sentence with “The next day is bright”. Before these words go into attention mechanism or transformer block, these are just input embeddings which has token + position embedding. So here each input embedding is an 8-dimensional vector (Assumed for simplicity) . Now I am going to show you how these embedding vecotrs are transformed into context vectors, the reason why we need to do this is because here if we see how much importance is carried for a particular word “day” amongst its neighboring words, it is not there. If we can integrate the importance of other words for each token, then there is a good chance of getting a context.

There are 3 trainable weitght matrices are **Query (Wq), Keys (Wk), Values (Wv)**. These 3 weight matrices are required in such a way that we kind of express the input embeddings into a different space which can capture the complex nuances which cannot be captured by a simple dot product. The initial values of these matrices are picked randomly and when the neural network trains itself by backpropogating , there is a chance of getting these values stabilized. The weight matrices row dimensions should match with column dimensions of input embeddings, and the column dimensions of these weight matrices can be anything; here we have assumed it to be 4.

We multiply the input embeddings of size (5\*8) with Wq (8\*4), the final query vectors size will be (5\*4).

Now we got the Q, K , V matrices with all numeric values. Now we need to calculate the attention scores which is done by multiplying Q and K.

So basically what we are getting by this attention scores is the importance of each word with other words.

The output with Query and Key values dot product will give us attention scores and it looks like this below. If we look at the second row in output — for the word “next” the corresponding attention scores for each of the other word is 0.1, 1.8, 0.6.0.1. 0.1 respectively. These values does NOT sum up to 1 and this is a problem to solve for us because we need to make some interpretable sentences using all these words by giving weightage to all the words and those weightages have to have a sum of 1. For e.g., for the word “next” we can have some percentage weightage for all the words as shown below.

So let us search for a method where we create probabilities that sum upto 1 and this is possible using softmax function on all the attention scores. for e.g., for the 2nd row in output of attention scores (for “next”)

but sometimes applying softmax also does not give a sum of 1 and hence a scaling mechanism is used before applying softmax.

## Why divide by SQRT(dimension Keys)

**Reason 1: For stability in learning**

The softmax function is sensitive to the magnitudes of its inputs. When the inputs are large, the differences between the exponential values of each input become much more pronounced. This causes the softmax output to become “peaky,” where the highest value receives almost gets all the probability mass, and the rest receive very little.

In attention mechanisms, particularly in transformers, if the dot products between query and key vectors become too large (like multiplying by factor of 8 in this example), the attention scores can become very large. This results in a very sharp softmax distribution, making the model overly too confident in one particular “key.” Such sharp distributions can make learning unstable.

**BUT WHY SQRT?**

**Reason 2:** To make the variance of the dot product stable

The dot product of Q and K increases the variance because multiplying two random numbers increases the variance.The increase in variance grows with the dimension.

Dividing by sqrt(dimension) keeps the variance close to 1.

> **Imagine you’re rolling dice. Consider two cases:**

**Case 1:** Rolling one standard die (1–6): The average (mean) is 3.5. The variance is relatively small (≈ 2.9). You have predictable outcomes.

**Case 2:** Rolling and summing 100 dice: The mean is 100 × 3.5 = 350. The variance significantly grows (100 × 2.9 = 290 10 x2.9 = 290). Now outcomes, More variability (e.g., you might get sums like 320, 350, or 380). The distribution spreads out drastically. Outcomes become unpredictable.

**Dot Product without normalization:**

Think of dimensions as “dice.” Increasing the number of dimensions is like rolling more dice and summing results. Each dimension (dice) contributes some variance. As dimensions grow, variance accumulates. **Result:** Dot products (before softmax) become either extremely large or small, making attention weights unstable and erratic.

**Dot Product with normalization (dividing by sqrt(d)):**

This effectively scales down the variance, ensuring the summed results remain stable. It’s like taking the average roll per dice rather than summing them up, stabilizing your expected outcomes. **Result:** Attention weights become more stable, predictable, and informative, enabling the model to learn effectively.

The attention weights obtained are multiplied with values matrix to get again context vectors.

For e.g., for the word “next” — the attention weights multiplied with values will give.

The final context vectors are shown here for the word “next” . The green arrows represent the scaled vectors with importance values and the final context vector is in orange color. Similarly all the context vectors obtained will differ from the input embeddings vector.

To finally summarize the entire process of getting in context vector is shown below . The “Self Attention” is the major block where we see all the magic happening with all weight matrices calculated with numeric calculations.

### **“Why the names Queries, Keys and Values?”**

The names have origins in the field of information retrieval.

1. You have a query: something you’re looking for.
2. The database has keys: tags or identifiers that help you match your query to relevant data.
3. Associated with these keys, you have values: the actual information or content you want to retrieve.

This analogy translates directly to attention mechanisms in neural networks:

**Query (Q):** The current word or token has a “question” about its context. It asks “What should I pay attention to?”

The code includes a note highlighting an advantage of using nn.Linear instead of manually implementing parameters: nn.Linear provides an optimized weight initialization scheme, which contributes to more stable and effective model training.

```
import torchimport torch.nn as nnclass SelfAttention_v2(nn.Module):    def __init__(self, d_in, d_out, qkv_bias=False):        super().__init__()        self.W_query = nn.Linear(d_in, d_out, bias=qkv_bias)        self.W_key = nn.Linear(d_in, d_out, bias=qkv_bias)        self.W_value = nn.Linear(d_in, d_out, bias=qkv_bias)            def forward(self, x):        keys = self.W_key(x)        queries = self.W_query(x)        values = self.W_value(x)                attn_scores = queries @ keys.T        attn_weights = torch.softmax(attn_scores / keys.shape[-1]**0.5, dim=-1)                context_vec = attn_weights @ values        return context_vec import import as class SelfAttention_v2 def __init__self, d_in, d_out, qkv_bias=False False super def forwardself, x 10.5 1 return
```

```
torch.manual_seed(789)inputs = torch.tensor(   [[0.43, 0.15, 0.89, 0.43, 0.15, 0.89, 0.1, 0.2], # The     (x1)    [0.55, 0.87, 0.66, 0.43, 0.15, 0.89, 0.1, 0.2], # Next (x2)    [0.57, 0.85, 0.64, 0.43, 0.15, 0.89, 0.1, 0.2], # day  (x3)    [0.22, 0.58, 0.33, 0.43, 0.15, 0.89, 0.1, 0.2], # is     (x4)    [0.77, 0.25, 0.10, 0.43, 0.15, 0.89, 0.1, 0.2] # Bright     (x5)   ] )d_in = 8d_out = 4sa_v2 = SelfAttention_v2(d_in, d_out)print(sa_v2(inputs)) 7890.430.150.890.430.150.890.10.2
# The     (x1)0.550.870.660.430.150.890.10.2
# Next (x2)0.570.850.640.430.150.890.10.2
# day  (x3)0.220.580.330.430.150.890.10.2
# is     (x4)0.770.250.100.430.150.890.10.2
# Bright     (x5) 8 4 print
```

This implementation follows the standard self-attention mechanism where:

* Input vectors are projected to queries, keys, and values using linear transformations
* Attention scores are computed as dot products between queries and keys
* Scores are scaled by dividing by the square root of the key dimension
* Softmax is applied to get attention weights

The final context vectors are computed by weighted sum of values.

So finally we learned **Self Attention** but to completely understand Multi-head Latent Attention we have to spend some more time.
